name: Day 1 — A Letter Written from Prison
focus_en: Who wrote this, where, and why does it matter?
reading_notes_en: Before diving into Ephesians, it helps to know who Paul was when he wrote it — a prisoner. Not a pastor in a comfortable office, but someone in chains. Yet the letter he writes from that cell is full of joy, vision, and hope. Read Acts 19 to get a feel for Paul's history with Ephesus — three years of ministry, healings, riots, a city turned upside down. The people receiving this letter knew Paul personally. Now he writes to remind them of the bigger picture — God's dream for the world. This letter is the blueprint for the New Human Project: what God is doing in and through Jesus to create a whole new kind of people.
key_idea_en: Ephesians isn't written to fix a problem. It's a vision — God's vision for a new humanity, laid out from a prison cell.
reflection_en: Paul writes with passion and hope despite being imprisoned. What does that say about where true hope comes from?
practice_en: Read Ephesians 1:1–2 slowly. Notice how Paul describes himself and his readers. What words stand out to you?

name: Day 2 — You Were Chosen Before the World Began
focus_en: God chose you — not because of what you've done, but because of his love.
reading_notes_en: Paul opens with an explosion of praise — blessing after blessing stacked on top of each other. And it all starts with this: God chose us before he made the world. Not after we proved ourselves. Not after we cleaned up our lives. Before any of it existed. The reason? Love. Pure and simple. The goal of being chosen is not to feel superior to others — it's to become holy and to be adopted into God's own family. This is where the new humanity begins: not with our effort, but with God's love reaching back before time itself.
key_idea_en: The new humanity starts with God's love, not our performance. You were chosen before you existed.
reflection_en: Does it feel hard to believe that God loves you unconditionally — before you do anything to earn it? Why or why not?
practice_en: Sit quietly for two minutes and let this sink in: God loved you before the world began. What emotions does that stir?

name: Day 3 — The Spirit as God's Down Payment
focus_en: What God has given us now — and the promise of so much more to come.
reading_notes_en: Paul keeps piling on the blessings: forgiveness, redemption, wisdom, and God's plan revealed to us. Then he introduces the Holy Spirit — and uses a fascinating word for him. The Spirit is God's 'deposit' or 'down payment' on our future. In Paul's day, a deposit was a legal commitment — a guarantee that the full payment was coming. The Spirit living inside you is not the whole of what God has planned. It's the first instalment — God saying: 'What I've started in you, I will finish.' Every member of the new humanity carries this guarantee inside them.
key_idea_en: The Holy Spirit in you today is God's guarantee that the best is still ahead.
reflection_en: When life feels uncertain or your faith feels weak, how does knowing the Spirit is God's guarantee help you?
practice_en: Read 1:13–14 again. Thank God specifically for the gift of his Spirit as a sign of what's coming.

name: Day 4 — Praying to Know God Better
focus_en: Paul's prayer — and why knowing God is better than knowing about God.
reading_notes_en: Paul prays for his readers — and what he asks for is striking. He doesn't pray for their comfort or safety. He prays that they would know God better. There's a difference between knowing facts about someone and actually knowing them. Paul wants the Ephesians to experience God's reality — to feel the hope of their calling, to grasp what God has given them, and to understand that the same power that raised Jesus from the dead is at work in them right now. People who know God this way are equipped to live as the new humanity in a world that doesn't know him.
key_idea_en: The most important thing you can pursue is knowing God himself — not just knowing things about him.
reflection_en: If someone could see your prayer life, would it look more like asking God to fix things, or asking to know him more deeply?
practice_en: Use Paul's prayer as your own today. Pray verses 17–19 out loud, replacing 'they' with 'I'.

name: Day 5 — Dead — But God
focus_en: The human condition, honestly described — and the grace that changes everything.
reading_notes_en: Paul gets honest here. Before Christ, we weren't just struggling — we were spiritually dead. Drifting. Following patterns and impulses that led nowhere good. But then comes verse 4 — two of the most important words in the Bible: 'But God.' Because of his great love, God stepped in. He didn't patch us up. He raised us to life. This is where the New Human Project begins for each of us personally — not with a resolution to do better, but with God breathing life into what was dead. The same power that raised Jesus from the grave is the power that woke you up spiritually.
key_idea_en: 'But God' — the two words that describe the moment grace interrupted everything and the new humanity became possible for you.
reflection_en: Can you identify your own 'but God' moment — when grace showed up and changed the direction of your life?
practice_en: Write down your 'but God' story in a few sentences. Keep it somewhere you can return to when faith feels difficult.

name: Day 6 — God's Masterpiece
focus_en: Saved by grace, not effort — and made for a purpose.
reading_notes_en: We are saved entirely by grace, through faith — not by anything we do. No room for boasting, no room for comparing ourselves to others. And yet verse 10 flips it. We are God's 'handiwork' — his masterpiece, created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared in advance for us to do. Works don't save us. But being saved changes us into people who naturally want to do good. This is the pattern for every member of the new humanity: received freely, lived out fully. You were rescued AND you were given a purpose.
key_idea_en: You didn't earn your place in God's new humanity — it was given to you. And that gift comes with a calling.
reflection_en: Do you tend to live more like you have to earn God's approval, or like you're free to serve from a place of already being loved?
practice_en: Ask yourself honestly: what good works do you sense God has prepared specifically for you? Write one down.

name: Day 7 — Remember Where You Came From
focus_en: Why Paul tells us to remember our past — and why that matters.
reading_notes_en: Paul says: remember. Remember what life was like without Christ — cut off from God's people, strangers to his promises, without hope, without God. Five negatives in two verses. Paul isn't trying to make us feel bad — he's setting the stage for us to feel the full weight of what God has done. You can't fully appreciate the rescue if you forget what you were rescued from. And you can't fully understand why the New Human Project is so remarkable if you forget the darkness it breaks into. Grace always looks biggest when you hold it next to what it replaced.
key_idea_en: Remembering where we came from keeps us humble, grateful, and full of wonder at what God has done.
reflection_en: What might you start taking for granted about your faith if you forget where you came from?
practice_en: Spend time today in genuine gratitude — not just for what God has given you, but for what he brought you out of.

name: Day 8 — One New Humanity
focus_en: The heart of Ephesians: Jesus has made enemies into family — and created something entirely new.
reading_notes_en: Jews and Gentiles were ancient enemies — divided by religion, history, and a literal wall in the Jerusalem temple that barred Gentiles from the inner courts. Paul says Jesus has torn that wall down in his own body on the cross. And he hasn't just brought two groups closer together. He has created one new humanity. Not Jews who tolerate Gentiles. Not Gentiles who adopt Jewish customs. Something brand new that only exists in Christ. This is the centrepiece of the whole letter — and the very phrase Paul uses. The church is not an organisation. It is a new humanity, called into existence by the cross.
key_idea_en: Jesus didn't just make peace between enemies. He created one new humanity — a community that couldn't exist without him.
reflection_en: Who are the people most unlike you that Jesus has called you into community with? What makes that hard?
practice_en: Think of one person in your church you rarely connect with. Make a small effort to reach out to them this week.

name: Day 9 — Everyone Gets Access
focus_en: Through Jesus, every person — regardless of background — comes to God the same way.
reading_notes_en: Verse 18 is quietly stunning: through Christ, by the Spirit, both groups now approach the Father. Same door. Same access. Nobody gets a VIP entrance. Nobody is turned away because of where they come from. The New Human Project has no second-class citizens — everyone enters through the same Jesus. And this access is not just vertical (us and God) — it's horizontal (us and each other). You can't genuinely claim to be close to God while keeping other people at arm's length. The two go together.
key_idea_en: There are no second-class citizens in God's new humanity. Everyone gets the same access, through the same Jesus.
reflection_en: Is there anyone you've treated — consciously or unconsciously — as less welcome in God's presence than you are?
practice_en: Meditate on the word 'access' today. You have been given an open invitation into the Father's presence. Use it.

name: Day 10 — You Are God's Home
focus_en: The church isn't a building where God visits — it's a community where God lives.
reading_notes_en: Paul gives the church three names in four verses: citizens, family, and a temple. That last image is the most striking. In the Old Testament, God's presence was located in the temple — a building. Now Paul says God lives in the community of believers. The new humanity, gathered around Jesus and his word, is where God has chosen to make his home. When people step into a church that is truly living as the new humanity — welcoming, honest, full of grace — they are stepping into the presence of God.
key_idea_en: The church is not a place you go to find God. It's the new humanity God has decided to live in.
reflection_en: Does your experience of church feel like a place where God is genuinely present? What contributes to that — or gets in the way?
practice_en: Next time you gather with your church family, arrive with this thought: 'God lives here.' See if it changes how you engage.

name: Day 11 — The Secret God Kept — Until Now
focus_en: The mystery revealed: the gospel is for everyone, and the church proves it.
reading_notes_en: Paul calls the inclusion of the Gentiles a 'mystery' — not something strange or secret, but something hidden in previous generations and now finally disclosed. The revelation is this: everyone is fully included in God's family, not as guests or second-tier members, but as fellow heirs. And the purpose goes even further — the church's unity is meant to be a message to the whole universe that God's reconciling plan is real. The new humanity is not just a community for its own sake. It is a living announcement that the New Human Project is underway.
key_idea_en: Your church's diversity is not a problem to manage. It's the point — a living demonstration to the world that the gospel works.
reflection_en: What does the mix of people in your church say about the gospel? What might it need to say more clearly?
practice_en: Pray for your church today — not for a program or event, but for genuine unity across every difference.

name: Day 12 — Love Too Big to Measure
focus_en: Paul's great prayer — and the love at the centre of the new humanity.
reading_notes_en: Paul kneels to pray — an unusual posture that signals the weight of what he's about to ask. He prays for three things: inner strength by the Spirit, Christ feeling at home in their hearts, and the capacity to grasp how wide and long and high and deep Christ's love is. Four dimensions — but the point isn't to measure it. The point is that it can't be measured. This love is not a warm feeling; it's the same love that drove Jesus to create the new humanity through the cross. To really know it is to be filled with the fullness of God himself.
key_idea_en: The new humanity is held together not by rules or culture, but by a love too big to get to the bottom of.
reflection_en: Do you find it easy or difficult to believe that God loves you personally and deeply? What gets in the way?
practice_en: Pray Paul's prayer for yourself today. Ask God to help you grasp — not just know about — how much you are loved.

name: Day 13 — More Than We Can Ask or Imagine
focus_en: The doxology at the heart of Ephesians — and why God's glory comes through the church.
reading_notes_en: Paul closes the first half of his letter with praise. God is able to do far more than we could ever ask or dream up — and this power is already at work inside us. And then this: glory comes to God 'in the church and in Christ Jesus.' Not just through individuals — through the church. When the new humanity lives together in a way the world can't explain, God is glorified. That's the vision. The New Human Project is God's greatest advertisement for his own character — a community of transformed people living as proof that Jesus is real.
key_idea_en: God is glorified when his new humanity lives together in a way that couldn't be explained without him.
reflection_en: What would your church look like if God did 'far more than you could ask or imagine' in and through it?
practice_en: Write a prayer asking God to do something in your church that feels impossible to you. Keep it and pray it for 25 days.

name: Day 14 — Now Live Like It
focus_en: The great hinge of the letter — from who you are to how you live.
reading_notes_en: One word bridges the two halves of this letter: 'therefore.' Everything Paul has said about who we are — chosen, loved, forgiven, made new, brought into the new humanity — now becomes the basis for how we live. This is not about earning God's approval. It's about living in a way that matches who you already are. Members of the new humanity don't live differently to become something — they live differently because of what they already are. Think of it this way: if you've been rescued, live like someone who's been rescued.
key_idea_en: You don't live the Christian life to join the new humanity. You live it because you're already in it.
reflection_en: Is there a gap between who you know yourself to be in Christ and how you actually live day to day? Where is that gap most obvious?
practice_en: Identify one area where you want to bring your behaviour into line with your identity in Christ. Name it. Pray about it.

name: Day 15 — Unity Is a Gift — Guard It
focus_en: The new humanity doesn't manufacture unity — it protects the unity the Spirit has already created.
reading_notes_en: Paul tells the church to 'make every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit.' Notice: keep, not create. Unity is already there — it's the Spirit's work. Our job is to protect it through humility, gentleness, patience, and bearing with one another in love. Then Paul lists seven things all believers share: one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father. These seven realities are deeper than any difference that might divide us. The new humanity is held together not by culture, personality, or politics — but by these seven unbreakable things.
key_idea_en: Unity in the new humanity isn't about everyone being the same. It's about letting what we share be bigger than what separates us.
reflection_en: Which of the seven 'ones' feels most under threat in your church or community right now?
practice_en: Choose one of the four attitudes Paul lists — humility, gentleness, patience, bearing with others — and focus on it intentionally today.

name: Day 16 — Everyone Has a Part to Play
focus_en: Gifts aren't for personal gain — they're for building the whole new humanity up.
reading_notes_en: Christ gave gifts to the church in the form of gifted people: apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers. Their job is not to do all the ministry themselves — it's to equip everyone else to do it. The new humanity grows when every member plays their part, not when a few professionals carry the whole load. The goal is maturity — a community so grounded in truth and love that it can't be knocked off course. Mature communities speak truth in love and build each other up. This is what the New Human Project looks like from the inside.
key_idea_en: The new humanity grows when everyone uses their gifts — not when a few people do everything.
reflection_en: What gift do you bring to the body of Christ? Are you using it — or has it been sitting on the shelf?
practice_en: Tell someone in your church this week what gift you see in them. And ask someone what gift they see in you.

name: Day 17 — Take Off the Old, Put On the New
focus_en: Real change starts in the mind — and works its way out into everyday behaviour.
reading_notes_en: Paul describes the old life as one with a darkened, hardened mindset — cut off from God, losing sensitivity to what's right. The new life begins with a renewed mind. We have to think differently before we live differently. Then Paul gets practical with real contrasts: stop lying, tell the truth. Don't let anger fester. Stop taking, start giving. Cut out talk that tears people down, speak words that build people up. Be kind. Forgive. These aren't suggestions for nice people — they are descriptions of what the new humanity actually looks like from Monday to Saturday.
key_idea_en: The new humanity isn't just a Sunday identity. It shows up in how you speak, handle conflict, and treat people every day.
reflection_en: Which of the contrasts Paul lists hits closest to home for you? What's one concrete step toward change?
practice_en: Pick one behaviour from this passage to work on this week. Tell someone you trust so they can help keep you accountable.

name: Day 18 — Copy God
focus_en: The extraordinary call to imitate God — and what that actually looks like in daily life.
reading_notes_en: 'Imitate God as dearly loved children.' It sounds audacious. But Paul immediately shows us what this means: walk in love, the way Christ loved us and gave himself up for us. The opposite of this is the world's pattern — grabbing, using, consuming — sexual immorality, greed, and coarse talk are all forms of taking rather than giving. And notice what Paul prescribes as the antidote: thanksgiving. Grateful people are not greedy people. Members of the new humanity are marked by generosity, because they know they've already been given everything they need.
key_idea_en: To be part of the new humanity is to love the way Jesus loved — generously, sacrificially, without keeping score.
reflection_en: In what relationship or situation do you find it hardest to love generously rather than keep score?
practice_en: Today, do one act of love for someone with no expectation of getting anything back. Do it quietly.

name: Day 19 — You Are Light — Live Like It
focus_en: You're not just living in the light — you actually are light. That changes everything.
reading_notes_en: Paul doesn't just say we live in the light now. He says we are light. Not a dim glow — actual light. And light does two things: it helps you see, and it exposes what's been hiding in the dark. Living as members of the new humanity means producing the fruit of light — goodness, righteousness, truth — and through contrast, naturally exposing what doesn't belong. This isn't about aggressive confrontation. It's about the simple power of a different kind of life. The new humanity shines not by announcing itself, but by simply being different.
key_idea_en: You don't have to announce you're part of the new humanity. A life lived in the light does its own announcing.
reflection_en: Where in your everyday life does your faith create genuine contrast with the culture around you? Where has it faded into the background?
practice_en: Ask God today: 'Where have I blended in when I should have stood out?' Listen honestly to the answer.

name: Day 20 — Be Filled with the Spirit
focus_en: The Spirit-filled life isn't a private experience — it overflows into how the new humanity lives together.
reading_notes_en: Paul compares being drunk with wine to being filled with the Spirit — both involve being 'under the influence' of something outside yourself. But the Spirit leads to wisdom, worship, and community. And the command is continuous: keep on being filled. This isn't a one-time event. The results are communal: singing together, giving thanks together, submitting to one another. Being filled with the Spirit doesn't just affect your private prayer life — it changes how you treat the people around you. The new humanity at its best is a Spirit-filled community that worships loudly and loves humbly.
key_idea_en: You can't be full of the Spirit and stay isolated. The Spirit always spills over into how we live together.
reflection_en: Is your experience of the Holy Spirit mostly private — or does it regularly overflow into how you treat and serve others?
practice_en: In your next church gathering, engage fully — sing like you mean it, thank God like you mean it, look for someone to encourage.

name: Day 21 — A Different Kind of Power
focus_en: The New Human Project changes how power works in every relationship.
reading_notes_en: Verse 21 is the heading over everything that follows: 'Submit to one another out of reverence for Christ.' Mutual submission is the governing principle of all relationships in the new humanity. When Paul talks about husbands and wives, he's not handing men power — he's calling them to a pattern of love modelled on Jesus, who served and gave himself for the church. That's a far cry from domination. In a world where authority meant power over others, the New Human Project reframes it completely. Authority in the new humanity looks like self-giving love. The question is never 'what can I demand?' but 'how can I serve?'
key_idea_en: The New Human Project doesn't give anyone permission to dominate. It calls everyone — especially the powerful — to serve.
reflection_en: In your closest relationships, do you tend to lead with your rights or with your responsibility to love? Be honest.
practice_en: In one relationship this week, look for an opportunity to serve before you seek to be served.

name: Day 22 — The Home as the First School
focus_en: The new humanity starts at home — in how we treat the people closest to us.
reading_notes_en: Paul addresses children directly — which was unusual in his time. Children were moral agents worthy of being spoken to, not just managed. He calls them to honour their parents. But he equally addresses fathers: don't exasperate your children. Don't provoke frustration through harsh or absent parenting. Instead, bring them up with nurture and guidance shaped by God's character. The home is the first classroom of the new humanity. How we treat the most vulnerable people in our homes reveals how deeply the New Human Project has actually taken root in us.
key_idea_en: The new humanity is practiced at home first — in how we treat the people we live with every single day.
reflection_en: What kind of atmosphere do you create at home? Do the people who live with you experience grace or pressure from you most often?
practice_en: If you have children or young people in your life, ask them how they feel around you. Then really listen to the answer.

name: Day 23 — Your Work Is Not Secular
focus_en: The New Human Project transforms how we see work — every workplace becomes a place of witness.
reading_notes_en: Paul addresses workers as people with dignity and moral agency, and gives them a framework that transforms every job: work 'as to the Lord.' When your ultimate employer is Jesus, no work is meaningless, no task beneath you. You're not just doing a job — you're offering your work to God. And for those with authority: remember you have a Master too, one who shows no favouritism. In God's new humanity, power is always held accountable. The New Human Project doesn't stay in the church building — it follows you into every meeting, every conversation, every task.
key_idea_en: There is no such thing as secular work for someone in the new humanity. Every job is an opportunity to serve Christ.
reflection_en: How would your attitude to your work shift this week if you genuinely approached it as service offered to Jesus?
practice_en: Before you start work today, offer it to God in a simple prayer. Notice if it changes how you feel about what you're doing.

name: Day 24 — Put On the Armour
focus_en: The new humanity is in a real battle — and God has already provided everything needed to stand firm.
reading_notes_en: Paul ends the letter where he began — with a cosmic perspective. The New Human Project faces real opposition. Behind broken systems, persistent temptation, and relentless discouragement, there are spiritual forces at work. So God gives us armour — but notice, it's mostly defensive. The call is to 'stand firm,' not to charge ahead alone. Truth, righteousness, the gospel of peace, faith, salvation, the Word of God — each piece is something God provides, not something we manufacture. And woven through all of it is prayer: pray at all times, for all the saints. The new humanity stands together or it doesn't stand at all.
key_idea_en: The new humanity is in a real battle — but not unarmed, and not alone.
reflection_en: Which piece of the armour do you most consistently forget to put on? What would it look like to do so deliberately each morning?
practice_en: Start tomorrow with a simple prayer that puts on each piece of armour. Use Ephesians 6:14–18 as your guide.

name: Day 25 — Grace, Peace — and a Life Forward
focus_en: Bringing it all together — what the New Human Project means for your life from here.
reading_notes_en: Paul closes by mentioning Tychicus — not a famous apostle, just a faithful friend who carried the letter and encouraged the churches in person. The new humanity is built by people like that. Ordinary, faithful, showing up. Now go back and read 1:3–14 again with everything you've learned. Four movements: New Life — you have been made alive in Christ. New Society — you've been brought into a community that crosses every divide. New Standards — you're called to live differently, starting in your mind. New Relationships — every relationship is now a place to practise the new humanity. This is the New Human Project. And you are part of it.
key_idea_en: The New Human Project is built by ordinary people who show up faithfully, love generously, and keep going.
reflection_en: After 25 days, what is the one thing from Ephesians you most want to carry into the rest of your life?
practice_en: Write down in your own words what the new humanity means to you. Share it with someone in your church this week.
